meta.title: How the Grammar Strategies Work
meta.description: Mechanism-by-mechanism explainer for the six grammar-lane strategies, each with a worked example from real ASR.

props.title: How the Strategies Work
props.subtitle: Six mechanisms, one shared idea: the grammar knows every legal command, so it can ask which legal command a garbled transcript is cheapest to explain as.
props.meta: Each section: plain mechanism · diagram · worked example from real ASR

props.content: The pilot already parses intent + slots from authority templates under measured noise. These six strategies extend it from a shadow observer into a recovery-and-promotion system. They all build on one core: the **priced repair model** below.

props.label: Foundation
props.title: The priced repair model (a noisy-channel decoder over your own templates)

props.content: ASR garbles a phrase the controller really said. The grammar holds every phrase that *can* be said (the authority rows). Repair search asks: **which legal phrase is this garble cheapest to explain as?** Cost is the evidence. Slot values are exact anchors — never invented; if a value won't resolve, the parse dies and we say so.

props.caption: Zero-cost normalizations first, then priced edits; slots are exact anchors; score-band decides the action.
props.content:
flowchart LR
  A["ASR text"] --> N["zero-cost normalize\ndehyphenate · homophones · numbers"]
  N --> C["Earley chart over 73 productions"]
  C --> E{"priced edits per token"}
  E -->|"match 0.0"| S["score = sum of edit costs"]
  E -->|"substitute ~0.35\nliterals only"| S
  E -->|"insert 0.6"| S
  E -->|"dup-collapse 0.2"| S
  E -->|"SLOT exact anchor\nresolve or die"| S
  S --> B{"score band"}
  B -->|"= 0"| H["normal candidate"]
  B -->|"0 to budget, slots bound"| R["candidate + repair evidence\nconfirmation readback"]
  B -->|"over budget"| SH["shadow only"]
  B -->|"slot unbound"| K["NO candidate\npartial names the slot"]

props.label: WORKED EXAMPLE
props.title: chain to MY frequency 124.5
props.content: One substitution `chain` to `change` (similarity-gated, cost 0.35), FREQUENCY binds exactly to 124.5. Lands in the **repaired band** -> candidate carries the repair in its ledger -> readback phrased for confirmation. The ledger *is* the corrected transcript, for free.

props.label: W1
props.title: Coverage dimension — the scoreboard every strategy is judged on

props.content: Not a new measurement — a **roll-up** of metrics that already exist. Every baseline row already carries `candidate_metrics` (which sources fired, agreement, critical-slot risk). W1 aggregates those into one run-level `grammar_coverage` block on every baseline, plus a display line. It reads row evidence; it never re-parses text. This is why it goes first: it is how we *see* whether the next five strategies actually moved anything.

props.caption: Per-row metrics already exist; W1 is the corpus-level sum.
props.content:
flowchart LR
  R1["row 1
candidate_metrics"] --> AGG["coverage.py
roll-up"]
  R2["row 2
candidate_metrics"] --> AGG
  R3["row N
candidate_metrics"] --> AGG
  AGG --> OUT["grammar_coverage
fired · clean/repaired · agreement
score-band · would-have-fixed"]
  OUT --> RR["RunReport
(every baseline)"]

props.label: TODAY'S NUMBERS
props.title: From the committed S5 receipt
props.content: 28 rows fired · 99 grammar candidates · 0 selected (shadow holds) · 93.6% corpus pass. W1 makes these a standing line on every run instead of a one-off replay.

props.label: W2
props.title: Island parsing — find the command, charge rent for the chatter around it

props.content: Today a production must match the **whole** segment (global alignment). The repair budget scales with row length, so a short command buried in verbiage can never afford to skip the surroundings. Island parsing prices leading/trailing out-of-span tokens on a **separate affix budget** (local alignment, Smith-Waterman style): find the command island, charge a small per-token rent for the neighborhood. Slots inside the island stay exact anchors.

props.caption: Global parse fails on the trailing clause; island parse pins the command and prices the affix.
props.content:
flowchart TD
  U["CONTACT GROUND ON 121.9 FOR PARKING INSTRUCTIONS"]
  U --> G["global parse\nwhole segment must match"]
  G --> GF["FAIL — budget exhausted\nby FOR PARKING INSTRUCTIONS"]
  U --> I["island parse"]
  I --> ISL["island: CONTACT GROUND ON 121.9\nFACILITY=ground FREQUENCY=121.9"]
  I --> AFF["trailing affix: FOR PARKING INSTRUCTIONS\npriced cheaply, separate budget"]
  ISL --> OK["accepted candidate\nslots bound"]

props.label: PINNED ROWS
props.title: Two real failing rows
props.content: `SQUAWK 4231` buried in ADIZ verbiage (leading affix) and `CONTACT GROUND … FOR PARKING INSTRUCTIONS` (trailing affix). Both fail today; both become accepted candidates after W2. Guard: the 3 never-promote rows must STILL stay out-of-band — recall must not buy false positives.

props.label: W4
props.title: Partial-evidence ranking — say which slot failed, not which row is longest

props.content: When no full parse exists, the parser still has partial parses. Today it ranks them by **furthest dot** — which just picks the longest production and reports an irrelevant terminal. The fix ranks by **slots bound + family plausibility**, so the top partial is the one a say-again consumer would act on: name the slot that's missing, or acknowledge the slot that's there.

props.caption: Same failed utterance, two rankings. Furthest-dot lies; slots-bound diagnoses.
props.content:
flowchart TD
  U["turn right heading 310 vector 4 visual approach"]
  U --> OLD["rank by furthest dot"]
  OLD --> OLDR["picks longest row\nsays expects: final"]
  OLDR --> BAD["useless say-again"]
  U --> NEW["rank by slots-bound + plausibility"]
  NEW --> NEWR["turn_direction_heading\nTURN_DIRECTION=right HEADING=310\nstalls on extra 'vector'"]
  NEWR --> GOOD["actionable: heading understood,\nconfirm the rest"]

props.label: REAL ASR FIXTURES
props.title: Mined from the transcript corpus
props.content: `turn right heading 310 vector 4 visual approach` -> today says **final**. `depart BAYVU heading 180` -> says **TURN_DIRECTION**. `maintain 3,000 until established on the SUNOL` -> says **the**. All garbage. Honest note: squawk has *no* real no-parse case (it's robust), so the synthetic `frew` string is the grounding fixture for the value-unresolvable case.

props.label: W5
props.title: Row promotion — turn one provably-correct row from shadow into selection authority

props.content: Today every grammar candidate is **shadow**: it appears in the trace but can never win selection. Promotion is one line of **policy data** — a `candidate_ids` allowlist entry — that flips a single template row from shadow to active. No code change, no selector change. Promotion unit is a row, not a family. Clean-band (score 0) only until the confirmation loop exists; the 3 mismatch rows are a permanent never-promote guard list.

props.caption: PolicyChain stays the only selector; the register is data it reads.
props.content:
flowchart TD
  GC["grammar candidates\n(all shadow today)"] --> PC{"PolicyChain\nthe only selector"}
  REG["60_grammar_promotion_register.yaml\ncandidate_ids allowlist"] --> PC
  GUARD["never_promote_guards.yaml\n3 mismatch rows"] --> PC
  PC -->|"row in register\n+ clean band"| SEL["SELECTED\n(real score bump)"]
  PC -->|"not registered"| SH["stays shadow"]
  PC -->|"guard-listed"| NV["never selected"]

props.label: FIRST THREE ROWS
props.title: Provably right where the pipeline is wrong
props.content: `turn_direction_heading` (0.0), `maintain_heading` (0.0), `monitor_facility_frequency` (0.0) — all parse clean while the pipeline fails them today. Proof gate: a same-session pre/post receipt pair shows ONLY these three flip; every other selected winner is byte-identical.

props.label: W3
props.title: Repair pricing as data — costs you can tune, not constants you must recompile

props.content: The repair op costs live as constants in scanner code today. W3 moves them to an authority data table with provenance per rule (rule_id, gate, cost, examples, source_report) — mirroring the existing `template_asr_cleanup` mined-rule lifecycle — plus per-family overrides and a new **containment** matcher (a short token inside a longer expected one, bounded char-diff and token-window). Loaded fail-closed. Now tuning is a loop: filter rules, replay the corpus, read the W1 coverage delta.

props.caption: Costs become governed, minable data; tuning is a measured loop.
props.content:
flowchart LR
  OLD["constants in scanner.py"] -.replaced by.-> TBL["grammar_repair_pricing.yaml\nrule_id · gate · cost · provenance"]
  TBL --> PORT["fail-closed loader port"]
  PORT --> SCAN["scanner prices edits"]
  SCAN --> RUN["just baseline"]
  RUN --> W1M["W1 coverage delta"]
  W1M -->|"tune rule"| TBL

props.label: PINNED ROW
props.title: Turn 30 D-E-G-R-E-S right
props.content: Dehyphenation yields `DEGRES`, which today no edit matches. W3 chains normalization INTO a priced substitution (`DEGRES` -> `DEGREES`), so the row parses. Fail-closed: a missing or invalid pricing table disables priced ops and reports a reason — never a silent fallback.

props.label: W6
props.title: Segmentation proposals — split by command shape, not keyword guesses

props.content: Today the compound splitter cuts on boundary **keywords** (contact, maintain, squawk…) gated by hand-curated rules — it is shape-blind. W6 runs island search per production and **tiles** the non-overlapping islands: each island arrives already parsed (intent + slots), so the split proposal and the candidates are the same object. It runs in **shadow** next to the keyword splitter — measured, never deciding splits — until the numbers earn it a promotion plan of its own.

props.caption: Keyword splitter asks where a command might end; island tiling asks what legal commands are present.
props.content:
flowchart TD
  U["turn left 270 descend and maintain 3000 contact tower 119.3"]
  U --> KW["keyword splitter\ncuts on contact/maintain\n(shape-blind)"]
  U --> IT["island tiling\nrun every production as local search"]
  IT --> I1["turn_direction_heading\nspan 1-3"]
  IT --> I2["descend_and_maintain\nspan 4-7"]
  IT --> I3["contact_facility_frequency\nspan 8-11"]
  I1 --> TILE["non-overlapping tiling\n= segmentation proposal\nwith intents attached"]
  I2 --> TILE
  I3 --> TILE
  TILE --> SH["SHADOW: measured vs keyword splitter\nno split decision changed"]

props.label: TARGET CLUSTER
props.title: The 15 compound failures
props.content: Compound is the single largest failing cluster. W6 measures island-tiling agreement vs the keyword splitter on all 15 — with `tiling_failed` counted in the denominator so the readiness number can't be inflated by survivor bias. Promotion of proposals into real splits is a separate, later plan.
